course_description: This course is about knowledge and reality. We will look at questions such as: Is anything known? Can we know anything independently of sense perception? Can we perceive mind-independent objects? Is there a mind-independent world? What are people? What is existence? What is causation? Questions will be examined from the perspectives of a variety of traditions, e.g., Greek and Hellenistic philosophy, modern philosophy, classical Indian philosophy, idealism, pragmatism, analytic philosophy and European philosophy.

incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims:
This course aims to:

(a)ﾠanalyse and evaluate some of the most important and interesting theses, both classical and contemporary, in epistemology and metaphysics; and

(b) examine the interplay of metaphysics and epistemology in the history of philosophy.

The course will enable participants to:

i. Recognise central issues in metaphysics and epistemology.

ii. Understand the way epistemological arguments have been employed to support metaphysical theses, (and vice-versa).

iii. Apply these notions to the comprehensions of philosophical texts.

iv. Recognise major trends in metaphysics and epistemology.

v. Discuss philosophical papers on the subject.

vi. Suggest original arguments or original perspectives concerning the topics discussed during the course.

learning_outcomes.description: Better appreciate fundamental issues in metaphysics and epistemology
Analyse problems and communicate with improved precision and clarity
Think critically, systemically, independently and creatively in analysing complex problems
Compare and assess opposing philosophical theses and assumptions
Better understand human experience and its relationship to the world

assessment_summary.title: Tutorial Participation
assessment_summary.weight: 20% 2 points for preparation and participation in each tutorial

assessment_summary.title: Essay
assessment_summary.weight: 45% 45 points for writing an essay (1800 words +/-10%)

assessment_details.title: In class summary and reflection
assessment_details.task_description: You will be required to summarize and reflect on the positions and arguments covered in the theory of knowledge part of the course (weeks 2-6). This will be done in writing during the scheduled tutorial time for week 7. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit in class
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .
assessment_details.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Tutorial Participation
assessment_details.weight: 20% 2 points for preparation and participation in each tutorial
assessment_details.task_description: Marks credited for weekly contributions to a community of philosophical inquiry in 10 tutorials. Tutorials run on weeks 2-13 excluding week 10. This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
If you cannot make a tutorial due to illness, you may present a medical certificate or an SAP. We can then meet during office hours for a discussion of the relevant lecture and reading, and we will discuss the text and lecture material together. No written substitute is available for the Tutorial Participation component of the assessment.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Essay
assessment_details.weight: 45% 45 points for writing an essay (1800 words +/-10%)
assessment_details.task_description: -Write 1800 words (+/-10%) on one topic from the reality part of the course. Topics will be suggested but you are also allowed to research and write on a question that particularly interests you and that you have discussed with the lecturer. See the assignment sheet on blackboard. -Whilst AI use is not prohibited, we strongly recommend that it not be used to write essays as the essay task will require students to critically engage in specific contexts for which AI provides only limited support and guidance. In summary, Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. -A word count that is within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. Word count does not include footnotes; however, discursive footnotes are included in the word count. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria. -Meticulously cite your sources, including by citing specific page numbers. If you are citing lecture material, cite specific lectures and slides. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Introduction Lecture, discussion and in-class activities introducing students to knowledge (epistemology) and reality (metaphysics). Readings/Ref: Week 1. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 2 To Week 12 (02 Mar - 24 May)
learning_activities.topic: Tutorials Tutorials occur during weeks 2-12 with the exception of week 10. All the tutorials with the exception of week 7 aim to discuss set reading. In week 7, we will have the written summary assessment.
Scepticism Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to scepticism Reading/Ref: Week 2. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Perception Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to perception Reading/Ref: Perception reading Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
The problem of induction Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to the problem of induction. Reading/Ref: Induction. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
A priori knowledge Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to a priori knowledge. Reading/Ref: A priori knowledge Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
The nature of knowledge Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to naturalized epistemology. Reading/Ref: Naturalised Epistemology Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Ontological Idealism Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to ontological idealism. Reading/Ref: Ontological Idealism Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Personal Identity Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to personal identity. Reading/Ref: Personal Identity Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
The Atman Debate Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to the Ᾱtman debate. Reading/Ref: the Ᾱtman debate Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Existence Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to the nature of existence. Reading/Ref: Existence. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Causation Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to the nature of causation. Reading/Ref: Causation. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
The possibility of metaphysics Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to the possibility of metaphysics Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Conclusion Lecture, discussion and in-class activities relating to the end of the course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
